macro_events.event_summary: Oil prices rise after the White House says an Iran ceasefire halts a 60-day war deadline, easing immediate war risk but keeping geopolitical premium in crude.
primary_drivers: Higher crude prices lift upstream E&P revenue and cash flow
Reduced tail risk of Strait of Hormuz disruption lowers shipping/insurance spike scenarios
Sustained geopolitical premium supports oil services capex outlook

beneficiaries.rationale: Largest US integrated with significant upstream leverage; ~$1 move in Brent translates directly to upstream EBITDA. Direct beneficiary of higher realized crude prices.
High-beta Permian pure-play E&P with substantial unhedged oil exposure; among the most rate-of-change sensitive names to WTI moves.
Diamondback is a near-pure Permian oil producer with low hedge ratios; cash flow scales almost linearly with WTI.
Largest oilfield services name with heavy Middle East exposure (Saudi, UAE). Sustained higher crude + reduced regional war risk supports international capex cycle thesis.
Oil services peer; rotates with SLB on crude price moves and Middle East activity outlook.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs; rising crude directly compresses airline margins. Delta has limited hedging vs peers.
Southwest's fuel cost structure is highly crude-sensitive after legacy hedges rolled off; near-pure domestic carrier with limited fare pricing power.
Same jet fuel cost passthrough mechanism; airline group typically sells off as a basket on crude spikes.
Ceasefire reduces near-term urgency for missile defense/munitions reorders that had been bid up on Iran war risk premium; defense primes typically fade on de-escalation headlines.
Patriot/SM-series and Tomahawk demand narrative weakens at the margin on ceasefire; defense basket rotation lower on de-escalation.

headlines: Oil rises as White House says Iran ceasefire halts 60-day war deadline
